title: Aligning for Specific Behaviors
concept: Alignment can tune specific model properties, like writing style, formatting choices, or persona tone, without changing world facts.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a primary goal of behavior alignment?

options.text: Making its tone polite and helpful
Adding new languages to its training data

exercises.explain: Alignment focuses on the style, safety, and presentation of the model's outputs, rather than feeding it new encyclopedic facts.

cards.title: Changing the Voice
cards.body: Did you know we can make an AI model speak like a Shakespearean poet or format every answer as a neat checklist, all without teaching it any new facts? Let's explore how we tune specific behaviors!

cards.title: Aligning Behaviors
cards.body: Specific behavior alignment is the process of training a model to adopt a particular voice, formatting style, or safety rule. It shapes *how* the model speaks, rather than *what* facts it knows about the world.

cards.title: The Flavoring Chef
cards.body: Think of a model's facts as the raw ingredients in a kitchen, and alignment as the chef's seasoning. Adding spicy pepper changes the taste and style of the dish, but it doesn't change the base ingredients (like chicken or potatoes) into something else. You are just styling the final plate!

cards.title: Pirate Recipes
cards.body: If you ask a standard model for a recipe, it might give you a boring list. After styling alignment, the model can give you the exact same recipe but written as a pirate: 'Arr! First, ye chop the onions, matey!' The facts of the recipe stay identical, but the behavior changes.

cards.title: The Retraining Myth
cards.body: Myth: Aligning a model to be polite and helpful requires completely retraining its entire brain from scratch. Reality: We just apply a styling layer on top of what it already knows! It keeps its core knowledge and simply learns a new way to express it.

cards.title: Same Facts, Different Styles
cards.body: See how the same factual knowledge about water (H2O) can be styled differently depending on the behavior alignment goals.

right.text: Howdy partner! That good old water is just two parts hydrogen and one part oxygen!

left.text: Factual Statement: Water consists of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.

exercises.prompt: What actually changes when we align a model for specific behaviors?

options.text: The core facts about the world change.
The tone and presentation style change.
The model forgets how to speak English.
The model becomes much slower at typing.

exercises.explain: Alignment changes how the information is packaged and delivered (tone, safety, length), but it doesn't change basic world facts like the shape of the Earth.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A model aligned to speak like a robot still knows the same factual information as a standard model.
exercises.explain: True! Alignment only alters the wrapper (how it talks, formats, or behaves), keeping the underlying factual database intact.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence about alignment goals.
exercises.sentence: Behavior alignment changes the tone and style of responses, but it does not change the facts, only the model's behavior and formatting.
exercises.explain: Alignment targets how the model behaves and presents its knowledge, such as its formatting or tone, while keeping the core facts unchanged.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is NOT an example of an AI model's aligned behavior style?

options.text: A helpful customer service agent that only uses bullet points.
A human user typing a query into a search engine.
A friendly coding assistant that explains concepts using emojis.

exercises.explain: AI models can be styled to act as characters (like robots) or format outputs strictly (like bullet points). Option B is a human role, not an AI behavior style.

exercises.prompt: When we align a model to speak like a '1920s detective', what actually happens to its underlying database of real-world facts?

options.text: It deletes all the facts the model learned before.
It changes the presentation style and tone without losing the facts.
It forces the model to lie and invent new facts.
It changes the programming language of the computer.

exercises.explain: Setting a persona or style during alignment changes the tone and output formatting, but the underlying core facts remain identical. This keeps the model accurate but highly customized!
